narrative: I understand how disappointing it must be to receive a rejection for your law school application, especially when it's based on a prediction about your future performance on the bar exam. Let me explain this in a clear and straightforward way, focusing on what led to this decision so you can make sense of it and think about your next steps. From what we know about your profile, you have a strong work ethic, as you're planning to attend full-time, and you come from an upper-middle-income family, which is a bit above average in our dataset. However, your academic indicators, like your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, are below the average of 3.21, and your LSAT score of 31 is lower than the typical 36.70, which might have raised some concerns for the model. Additionally, you're in the 1st decile for your first-year grades and the 2nd decile for your third-year grades, meaning you're ranking lower than most students in those areas based on school performance metrics. The model, which looks at patterns from past students to predict bar exam outcomes, estimated a 67.9% chance that you might fail the exam on your first try, and because of that, your application was unfortunately rejected—it essentially sees this as a risk that could affect your success in law school. When we break down the factors that influenced this prediction, the most important one was your third-year decile ranking, as it strongly pushed the model toward predicting failure by highlighting how your grades in that year were below expectations compared to others. Right after that, your first-year decile played a significant role, also contributing to the failure prediction because it showed your early academic performance was on the lower end. Your LSAT score was another key influence, pushing the prediction further toward failure since it was notably below average and is often a strong indicator of readiness for legal studies. Then, your racial background was considered important, as it factored into the model's assessment in a way that aligned with the prediction of challenges ahead, based on broader trends in the data. Following that, your family income bracket had some influence, contributing to the failure outlook, possibly because it didn't stand out as a major advantage in this context. Your undergraduate GPA also played a part, adding to the prediction of failure due to its below-average value, which the model links to overall academic preparedness. In terms of the less influential factors, your full-time status and gender were at the bottom of the list but still nudged the prediction slightly toward failure. Overall, these features were ranked by their impact on the decision, with your third-year decile being the top concern and gender being the least significant among them—all of them pointing in the same direction, toward a higher risk of not passing the bar exam. It's important to remember that this is just one prediction based on the information available, and it doesn't define your potential. Moving forward, you might focus on improving your grades or retaking the LSAT to boost your scores, as these are areas you can work on through additional study or academic support. If possible, seeking mentorship or resources to strengthen your performance in key subjects could make a big difference for future applications. We're here to support you in understanding this process, and I encourage you to use this feedback as a stepping stone toward achieving your goals.
